response: Decision recorded above: cap set at 300/key/day, with a dated 60-day-post-launch trigger to check hit-rate and conversion vs. churn before adjusting further. Saved the rationale to project memory for future sessions in this repo.

premortem.response:
Premortem — three plausible failure classes, each grounded in specifics from `MEMO.md` and the CSV:

**1. The cap was anchored to what got built, not to demand — so it never bites.** The 1,000/day figure wasn't derived from usage; it's the number picked "because it felt generous" and then load-tested (MEMO.md:17-18). The memo already forecasts the failure mode: at that ceiling the free tier "covers essentially every real free user's entire usage" (MEMO.md:29), given even the busiest observed day stays under a 250/key average (MEMO.md:24). Six months out, this looks like: free-tier hosting cost keeps accruing with no conversion pressure, upgrade signups stall, and the founder eventually has to retroactively lower a public number — which, for users who built workflows assuming 1,000/day headroom, reads as a bait-and-switch, with no support team to absorb the backlash.

**2. A cap set from the pre-billing usage snapshot gets outrun by real usage.** The CSV covers 30 days (2026-05-29 to 2026-06-27) before Pro existed, when nobody had a reason to economize or push volume. A cap set near that band (Option B's ~150/day) is calibrated to casual, unpriced behavior — not to what happens once someone builds an actual integration expecting the free tier to hold, or a batch/demo spike that's spikier than the sampled range. With no account team (this is a solo founder, MEMO.md:3), one loud "I got walled off mid-workflow" complaint is disproportionately costly to contain.

**3. The conversion-lever logic was never validated, so the wrong causal bet gets made at any number.** The memo says outright that "how strongly the cap drives upgrades is a guess, and it is the guess the entire decision rides on" (MEMO.md:36-37) — there's no Pro usage data yet because Pro isn't live. If the real relationship is "hit the wall → churn/switch to a competitor" rather than "hit the wall → upgrade" (plausible for a low-switching-cost category like markdown-to-PDF), then tightening the cap doesn't grow Pro revenue, it just shrinks the active-key count. This failure wouldn't show up as "the number was wrong" — it'd show up as flat Pro signups and declining free-tier activity six months later, with no clean signal on which knob to turn next.

assumption.response:
Options and their single load-bearing dependency:

- **A: cap at 1,000/day** (matches build, costs nothing extra). Load-bearing premise: that engineering convenience should govern a conversion lever at all — i.e., that the cap's job is just "don't strain infra," not "drive upgrades." If X is false — if the cap is meant to actually gate usage and nudge conversion, not just sit safely under load-test limits — option A flips, since at 1,000/day it "covers essentially every real free user's entire usage" (MEMO.md) and nobody converts.

- **B: cap near observed usage (~150/day)**. Load-bearing premise: that the cap materially drives free→Pro conversion — the memo's own framing ("Set it near the typical day and active users hit it within a few weeks, and some of them convert"). If X is false — if users who hit a tight cap mostly churn instead of upgrading (plausible, since "I have no conversion data yet... it is the guess the entire decision rides on") — option B flips, since squeezing casual free users then just loses them rather than converting them.

- **C: middle ground, revisit after Pro has run**. Load-bearing premise: that the founder will actually revisit the number once real conversion data exists — i.e., that "temporary" cap is really temporary and not a de facto permanent setting by default/inertia. If X is false — if there's no concrete trigger or date to reopen the decision — option C flips into just being a worse version of A or B (arbitrary number, no plan behind the "plan to move it").
